message:
perf(pager): reuse canonical single-batch payload in group-commit merge

`merge_prepared_group_commit_batches` was unconditionally walking the
slice of staged WAL batches and synthesizing a new merged payload —
even when the slice had a single entry that already represented a
clean, canonical single-commit batch. In that fast-path case the
merge does no useful work: it copies frames into a fresh `Vec`,
re-runs the running-checksum recomputation, and rebuilds the metadata
that the input already carried. Group-commit calls this on every
publish, so the wasted work showed up as a fixed per-publish tax for
single-writer workloads (the common case during contention-free
periods between sustained-write storms).

Add a single-batch fast path:

  - `prepared_batch_is_canonical_single_commit(prepared, final_db_size)`
    checks that the prepared batch contains EXACTLY one frame whose
    `db_size_if_commit` is non-zero (the "this is a commit frame"
    marker), that this frame is the LAST frame in the batch, that its
    in-frame `db_size` header matches `final_db_size`, that
    `last_commit_frame_offset` agrees, and that `db_size_if_commit ==
    final_db_size`. All conditions must hold for the prepared payload
    to be byte-identical to what the merge would produce.
  - When the slice has length 1 and the canonical predicate holds,
    return `prepared.clone()` directly. `PreparedWalFrameBatch` is
    cheap to clone (its frames are stored in an `Arc`-backed buffer);
    the clone is structurally a refcount bump on the frame storage
    plus copies of the small metadata vectors, far cheaper than
    rebuilding the whole payload.
  - Any deviation from canonical (multiple commit markers, commit
    frame not in tail position, header/db_size mismatch, frame size
    smaller than the 8-byte header read window) falls through to the
    existing merge path. The fall-through is the conservative choice:
    if we can't prove the input is canonical, recompute.

The new test
`test_parallel_wal_prepared_merge_reuses_canonical_single_batch`
constructs a two-frame staged batch (one non-commit frame plus a
canonical commit frame at the tail), pre-populates
`finalized_for` and `finalized_running_checksum` so we can detect
mutation, and asserts that the merge returns a payload byte-equal to
the input AND that the prelock finalization survives unchanged. The
case-prefixed assertion messages match the existing convention in
this test module.

No behavioral change for slices with > 1 batch or for non-canonical
single batches; those paths still go through the same merge code as
before.
